title: Will Smith’s ‘Aladdin’: Why Does This Movie Exist? | Exit Survey
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins exit-survey Guy Ritchie's Aladdin remake, weighing Will Smith's blue-genie CGI against his human-body charm, then zoom out to ask why Disney is remaking its whole animated library at once.

films.title: Aladdin
films.note: Guy Ritchie's live-action remake, which Dobbins says left her not sure what movies are anymore. It plays like a music video crossed with a nostalgia machine, with a money grab underneath. Will Smith splits the role: charming and funny in his own body, tougher to buy as the giant blue CGI genie, where the shadow of Robin Williams sits heavy. Naomi Scott's Jasmine gets a new song, "Speechless," that both hosts agree the movie doesn't earn. Mena Massoud, Marwan Kenzari, and Nasim Pedrad all hold their own next to Will Smith. Fennessey clocks it as the closest Smith has gotten to his 1989 rapper persona on screen. Ends on a Will Smith/DJ Khaled credits remix of "Friend Like Me" that leaves them both horrified and delighted.

films.title: Aladdin (1992)
films.note: The animated original, credited to 16 different story writers, which both hosts say has aged well. Robin Williams's Genie could morph into Johnny Carson mid-scene without it feeling strange; the 2019 version restages entire sequences, like the opening chase, almost shot for shot.

films.title: Mamma Mia!
films.note: Dobbins's reference point for the nostalgia-jukebox feeling Aladdin is chasing. Thanks to Meryl Streep and Pierce Brosnan's "earned seriousness," it stays on the right side of embarrassment more successfully.

films.title: Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again
films.note: The other time Dobbins felt the exact whiplash between joy and secondhand embarrassment that Aladdin gave her.

segments.heading: Guy Ritchie's filmography
segments.note: how he ended up directing Aladdin

films.title: Lock, Stock and Two Smoking Barrels
films.note: Where Ritchie started: British gangster movies Fennessey says still hold up.

films.title: Swept Away
films.note: His remake of the Lina Wertmüller classic, starring his then-wife Madonna. Destroyed by critics, seen by almost no one.

films.title: Revolver
films.note: An attempt to get back to his gangster-movie lane.

films.title: RocknRolla
films.note: Same lane. Fennessey calls both this and Revolver relatively anonymous by Ritchie standards.

films.title: Sherlock Holmes
films.note: Fennessey says he likes Robert Downey Jr., Jude Law, and Ritchie individually and still doesn't respond to these movies at all.

films.title: Sherlock Holmes: A Game of Shadows
The Man from U.N.C.L.E.
films.note: Fennessey's actual favorite of the bunch (Armie Hammer and Henry Cavill in great suits, Hugh Grant on a raft at the end), despite it not finding an audience.

films.title: King Arthur: Legend of the Sword
films.note: Charlie Hunnam as King Arthur, reworked into something closer to a Jackie Chan movie. Flopped hard, which made his Aladdin hiring a surprise to both hosts.

films.title: The Gentlemen
films.note: Ritchie's next movie, a return to the British-gangster register he started in.

films.title: 101 Dalmatians
films.note: Glenn Close, the wave's earliest entry.

films.title: Maleficent
films.note: The five-year run of these movies effectively starts here, per Fennessey.

films.title: The Jungle Book
films.note: Jon Favreau, whose Lion King is coming next.

films.title: Pete's Dragon
films.note: David Lowery's version, which Fennessey holds up as the rare one that actually reimagined its source rather than just restaging it, though it made nowhere near the money the others did.

films.title: Beauty and the Beast
films.note: Bill Condon's version. A huge hit that Fennessey says has nothing to say about anything.

films.title: Christopher Robin
films.note: The Winnie the Pooh reimagining, also nowhere near as financially successful as the marquee remakes.

films.title: Dumbo
films.note: Tim Burton's version, which neither host saw. Fennessey calls its underperformance against its high expectations a turning point that exposed a weakness in the whole strategy.

films.title: The Lion King
films.note: Favreau's follow-up to Jungle Book, out in July; Billy Eichner is playing Timon or Pumbaa.

films.title: Maleficent: Mistress of Evil
films.note: The sequel, out in October.

films.title: Lady and the Tramp
films.note: Going straight to Disney+ in November instead of theaters.

films.title: Mulan
films.note: Directed by Niki Caro, whom Fennessey notes would make it the first of these remakes directed by a woman. He thinks the material actually justifies a live-action treatment.

films.title: Cruella
films.note: A 101 Dalmatians prequel starring Emma Stone.

segments.heading: The Disney classics behind all of it
segments.note: the animated originals name-checked along the way

films.title: Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs
films.note: Cited as the start of the sleepy-princess lineage the hosts joke about.

films.title: Sleeping Beauty
films.note: Home to Maleficent, whom Dobbins says scared her as a kid the way Ursula did.

films.title: The Little Mermaid
films.note: One of the Disney Golden Age titles Dobbins name-checks alongside Beauty and the Beast and The Lion King.

films.title: Beauty and the Beast (1991)
films.note: The Golden Age original, about Belle rather than a supporting player, which Dobbins contrasts with Aladdin's original Jasmine being sidelined in her own movie.

films.title: The Lion King (1994)
films.note: The other Golden Age title Dobbins was blasting on her way into the studio, alongside Aladdin's soundtrack.

films: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker

referenced.group: Disney/Pixar's own original work, for contrast
